---
title: "Knowledge, expertise, experience"
description: "Vanilla RAG lives at the knowledge layer; a wiki-plus-LLM substitutes for an advisor only when it also carries the author's judgment and grounded cases."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [research-agents]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Framing.** A descriptive synthesis, and it names a tooling gap that is not yet built.
:::

**Thesis.** An LLM-authored wiki can capture three separable things, and most systems capture only the first.

**Knowledge** is facts: what things are, how they relate. **Expertise** is judgment: which tradeoffs matter, what to prefer, what to distrust. **Experience** is the concrete cases behind the abstractions: what was tried, what worked, what failed, which dead ends are not worth revisiting. The three are separable but not hierarchical.

Vanilla RAG operates almost entirely at the knowledge layer. "Your files are searchable" is a knowledge claim. A reading list carries knowledge too. But a good graduate advisor adds expertise and experience on top of the reading list, and that is exactly the difference between a textbook and an advisor. A wiki that records all three is closer to the advisor. The wiki should ship compiled competence, not just searchable content.

The three-layer framing is a descriptive synthesis, not a term of art; it lines up with several existing frameworks at once (declarative versus procedural knowledge, the tacit-explicit distinction, case-based reasoning), which is why we resisted coining an acronym for it. It cuts across those axes rather than sitting neatly on any one.

The practical finding is that the **experience layer is the thinnest**, across every wiki we have looked at, relative to what it could hold. Failed approaches and grounded cases are exactly what a group's tacit memory should preserve, and exactly what tends not to get written down. That is the largest tooling opportunity here: LLM-assisted authoring that captures expertise and experience as they happen, rather than hoping someone writes them up later. It does not exist yet.

This framing is orthogonal to the retrieval work. Retrieval decides whether the LLM finds the right pages. These three layers decide whether the right pages were worth finding.
